That means separate admin UIs, separate role management, and separate provisioning workflows - disconnected from where enterprises already govern their identities. For customers running multiple SAP cloud services, this multiplies overhead and creates governance blind spots.

    Our direction is clear: as part of SAP's broader platform strategy, SAP cloud services will no longer maintain their own user and authorization management. Everything consolidates into SAP Cloud Identity Services (SCI) - the single identity fabric across the SAP cloud portfolio.

    This transition will happen in two phases:

    • Phase 1: New SAP Cloud ALM tenants will be provisioned directly with SCI and AMS from the start — no legacy layer, no migration needed.
    • Phase 2: Existing customers will follow once the migration tooling and transition paths are in place. We will not ask customers to migrate without a clear, supported path.

      For SAP Cloud ALM customers, this transition means:

      • One place to manage users - no more context-switching between application-specific admin interfaces
      • Centralized authorization governance in SCI  - consistent, auditable, and aligned with enterprise-wide identity policies
      • Seamless corporate IdP integration - users synchronized into SCI regardless of whether a customer relies on SAP's identity provider or their own corporate identity provider
      • Readiness for SAP Joule - the SCI migration is an enablement for bringing SAP's AI copilot capabilities into SAP Cloud ALM

        This is not just a technical migration. It is the prerequisite for the next horizon — and the realization of an integrated SAP ALM Suite where identity is handled once, consistently, and at scale.

        A note on ACLs during the transition: SAP Cloud ALM's Landscape Management Access Control already provides a form of attribute-based access control today — allowing fine-grained restrictions on which landscape objects (systems, services, business services) individual users can see and modify. This capability will remain fully available throughout the transition period. ACLs are not replaced by Horizon 2; they continue alongside the new identity foundation until Horizon 3 introduces a unified, policy-driven approach across all authorization dimensions.

        Horizon 3 - Attribute-Based Authorization via SAP Cloud Identity Services AMS

        With SAP Cloud Identity Services in place as the central identity foundation, the third horizon becomes possible: authorization that goes beyond roles and reflects the full organizational context of the user.

        Fine-granular role templates answer the question of what someone can do. This horizon answers the harder question: under what conditions can they do it.

        Restricting access based on context - limiting a user to a specific process area, a defined set of scenarios, or an organizational scope - requires a fundamentally different technical approach. Role-based access control alone cannot model this complexity at enterprise scale. This capability is unlocked by migrating to SAP Cloud Identity Services Authorization Management Service (AMS) - a modern, policy-driven framework for Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC). AMS policies evaluate not just who you are, but what you are accessing and under what organizational context. Critically, AMS builds directly on the SCI foundation established in Horizon 2 - it cannot be introduced without it.

        With AMS, SAP Cloud ALM will unlock:

        • Process area- and scenario-scoped authorization - users see and interact with only the parts of SAP Cloud ALM relevant to their responsibilities
        • Context-aware access policies - dynamic, fine-grained control that scales with organizational complexity
        • A future-proof authorization architecture - fully aligned with SAP BTP standards, consistent across SAP cloud services

          This is the horizon where authorization becomes a true enterprise governance instrument - not a set of switches to toggle, but a policy layer that reflects how your organization actually operates.

          Why This Matters Now?

          The shift to cloud-native ALM is accelerating. Customers are consolidating their SAP landscapes, expanding their use of SAP Cloud ALM across more processes and teams, and increasing their expectations for governance and compliance. The authorization model must keep pace.

          At the same time, the broader industry is moving decisively toward attribute-based, policy-driven access control - away from the rigid role hierarchies of the past. SAP Cloud ALM's path through these three horizons is not just a product improvement roadmap. It is alignment with where enterprise identity and access management is heading and a commitment to making SAP Cloud ALM ready for the demands of the next decade.

          Summary

          SAP Cloud ALM's authorization model is undergoing a fundamental, three-horizon transformation

          • Horizon 1 (later this year): Fine-granular role templates and custom role collections - giving enterprises the flexibility to model authorization to their organizational structure
          • Horizon 2: Migration to SAP Cloud Identity Services AMS - enabling attribute-based authorization, including process area- and scenario-scoped access control
          • Horizon 3: Full consolidation into SAP Cloud Identity Services - a single identity fabric across the SAP cloud portfolio, a prerequisite for SAP Joule integration
          • Together, these horizons move authorization from an administrative necessity to a strategic enterprise capability

            Related Resources

            • SAP Cloud ALM Role Collections – SAP Help Portal
            • Roles Setup Guide for SAP Cloud ALM
            • Centralize Authorization Management of SAP Cloud ALM Using the Identity Provisioning Service
            • SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Provisioning
            • Corporate Identity Providers in SAP Cloud Identity Services

entry.title: Joule A2A: Async Push Notifications for Long-Running Agents
entry.content:
In my previous post on multi-turn conversations with Joule, I showed how an A2A agent can use contextId and taskId to ask follow-up questions and stay in the conversation across turns. That works beautifully — as long as your agent answers within the synchronous request/response window.

But what happens if your agent needs to think for longer? A long-running calculation, a slow downstream system, a tool call that takes minutes, a human-in-the-loop approval step? With purely synchronous A2A, Joule waits — and eventually times out. This post covers the missing piece: asynchronous A2A with push notifications, where your agent acknowledges the request immediately and pushes the result back to Joule whenever it's actually ready.

The flow works, the tokens line up, and the result lands in the Joule UI as a proper assistant message. Below is the complete setup — from why you'd want this, through the IAS App2App configuration, to the Python code that ties it all together.

Why async?

Synchronous A2A is the default — Joule sends a request to your agent, your agent processes it, returns the result, Joule shows it. Simple. But the model breaks down in a few common scenarios:

  • Long-running tools. Your agent calls a backend that takes 30+ seconds to respond. The HTTP connection between Joule and your agent shouldn't have to stay open that long.
  • Multi-step orchestration with waits. Your agent kicks off a workflow, polls for completion, then synthesizes a result. Total time: minutes, not seconds.
  • Human-in-the-loop. Your agent needs an approval from someone external before it can finalize the answer. That "someone" might take an hour to click the button.

In all of these cases, you want your agent to say "got it, working on it" right away, free up the connection, and push the actual result back later. That's exactly what A2A push notifications give you, and Joule supports it on the orchestrator side.

 

High Level Flow

User
↓
Joule
↓
Your Agent (CF App)
↓
Ack the request immediately (status: working)
↓
Process asynchronously (seconds, minutes, ...)
↓
Exchange the inbound user token (JWT-bearer grant)
for a JWT scoped to the Joule async API via Destination Service
↓
POST callback URL with the final result
↓
Joule receives async response → User sees it in the UI

It all starts with the inbound call from Joule to the agent — this is where we get the user token to hand over. The HTTP call is acknowledged by our agent — basically saying "just a sec, response is coming". And finally our agent can use Joule's callback API: given the correlation ID, the conversation ID and our user token, it lets us send a message to that specific conversation, and it appears for the user.

Setup

Prerequisites

The async setup builds on two things: first, we need a working A2A-speaking agent — I recommend you follow the first A2A post for that. In addition we need the logged-in user's token — for that I recommend checking out the post on principal propagation. For simplicity I will recap the relevant steps here as well.

Plus there are some general prerequisites:

  • Access to the IAS Admin Console of the tenant your Joule instance trusts.
  • The right user roles: extensibility_developer and capability_admin to deploy capabilities, plus end_user to chat with Joule.

IAS App2App configuration

Now let's start with the crucial configuration on the IAS side. Because the callback API requires us to authenticate, we need to set up some IAS dependencies to make that work.

Create an IAS application for the agent (I called mine A2A_Custom_Agent_App). This is the OAuth client that will request the exchanged JWT.

Add a dependency on that application pointing at the Joule async API. The dependency name is up to you — pick something descriptive (I used agent-to-joule). What matters is that the dependency targets the Joule API; that's what makes the exchanged JWT a valid credential for the callback URL. Whatever name you pick here is the same one that ends up in the destination's tokenService.body.resource field below.

Create a client secret on the custom agent application under Client Authentication → Add. Save it — IAS only shows it once.

 

The credentials shown after creation are the clientId / clientSecret you'll plug into the destination:

 

The values you'll keep around for the destination configuration:

What Where it comes from

Token Service URLhttps://<your-ias-tenant>.accounts.ondemand.com/oauth2/token
Client IDClient ID of the IAS app you created in step 1
Client SecretClient secret from step 3
Dependency nameThe dependency you added in step 2 (e.g. agent-to-joule)
JWKS URIhttps://<your-ias-tenant>.accounts.ondemand.com/oauth2/certs

Capability change: enable push notifications

To enable push notifications you need to make a small change in the YAML. It needs one extra flag in the agent request action to tell Joule it should expect an async response from this agent:

# functions/currency_agent_function.yaml
action_groups:
  - actions:
      - type: agent-request
        system_alias: CURRENCY_AGENT
        agent_type: remote
        body:
          contextId: "<? agent_context_id ?>"
          taskId: "<? agent_task_id ?>"
          configurations:
            pushNotifications: true   # <-- the only change vs. the sync setup
        result_variable: "_agent_response"

With pushNotifications: true, Joule does two things differently:

  • It sends a pushNotificationConfig block in the request body, containing the callback URL your agent is expected to POST back to.
  • It accepts an immediate working status from your agent without timing out, and waits for the eventual callback.

The rest of the capability — the scenario, the capability_context for agent_context_id / agent_task_id, the response routing for input-required vs. completed — stays identical to the multi-turn setup. Async composes cleanly with multi-turn.

What the agent receives

When push notifications are enabled, the request from Joule to your agent now includes a pushNotificationConfig:

{
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "id": "6967bf3e-a16e-4d06-93d2-2fc900dad7cb",
  "method": "message/send",
  "params": {
    "message": {
      "role": "user",
      "parts": [{"text": "convert 5 usd to eur", "kind": "text"}],
      "messageId": "6967bf3e-a16e-4d06-93d2-2fc900dad7cb",
      "contextId": "ce5e0959-fc18-4921-b7fa-8d34084b91e2",
      "kind": "message"
    },
    "configuration": {
      "pushNotificationConfig": {
        "url": "https://sapdas.<region>.sapdas.cloud.sap/api/agent/v1/callback/a2a"
      }
    }
  }
}

Three pieces of information your agent needs to keep around for the callback:

  • The callback URL from params.configuration.pushNotificationConfig.url.
  • The inbound Authorization: Bearer <token> header — this user token gets exchanged for a JWT scoped to the Joule async API.
  • The HTTP headers conversationid and x-correlationid — these travel with the original request and must be echoed back on the callback so Joule can correlate the async result with the right conversation.

The token flow

Two destinations are needed: one from Joule to the custom agent, and one for the way back from our agent to Joule's callback API.

Inbound: how Joule reaches the agent

Joule itself uses a destination to call the agent. That destination lives in the Joule subaccount and is the entry point for every A2A request. It's an OAuth2JWTBearer destination — same authentication type we'll use on our side — pointed at the agent's Cloud Foundry URL, with the A2A proxy app's credentials. I call it the CURRENCY_AGENT destination here, but it could be any name.

 

 

Note: these credentials and the proxy app are created in the previous blog — please refer to part 1 and 2 of my principal propagation blog post.

Outbound: how the agent calls Joule back

To call Joule's async callback, we need a JWT that has access to the Joule API. This goes through the Joule IAS app. The interesting pattern here: the Joule das-ias IAS app is SAP-owned/managed, which means we cannot create client credentials on it. Instead we use our own app and create a dependency on it. That's where the A2A custom agent app comes in — from the inbound flow we get a token for that A2A custom agent IAS app, and we need to exchange it for one valid against the Joule app.

There are two ways to do that exchange: do it yourself in the agent, or let the BTP Destination Service handle it. We'll go with the destination service: less code, IAS credentials stay out of the agent's environment, and rotating the secret is easier.

The destination

In the BTP cockpit (or via the destination-configuration REST API), create an HTTP destination with Authentication: OAuth2JWTBearer. The full JSON shape:

{
  "Name": "JOULE_ASYNC_API",
  "Type": "HTTP",
  "URL": "https://sapdas.<region>.sapdas.cloud.sap/api/agent/v1/callback/a2a",
  "ProxyType": "Internet",
  "Authentication": "OAuth2JWTBearer",
  "tokenServiceURL": "https://<your-ias-tenant>.accounts.ondemand.com/oauth2/token",
  "tokenServiceURLType": "Dedicated",
  "clientId": "<APP2APP_CLIENT_ID>",
  "clientSecret": "<APP2APP_CLIENT_SECRET>",
  "tokenService.body.resource": "urn:sap:identity:application:provider:name:agent-to-joule",
  "tokenService.body.token_format": "jwt",
  "tokenService.addClientCredentialsInBody": "true",
  "x_user_token.jwks_uri": "https://<your-ias-tenant>.accounts.ondemand.com/oauth2/certs",
  "HTML5.DynamicDestination": "true"
}

 

 

A few of these fields are not optional — they are exactly the difference between a working destination and a generic Retrieval of OAuthToken failed due to: unexpected error:

Field Why it has to be there

tokenService.body.resourceThe audience of the exchanged JWT — must be urn:sap:identity:application:provider:name:<APP2APP_DEPENDENCY> (or the clientid URN variant). Without it the destination service has no idea which provider app to exchange against.
tokenService.body.token_format = "jwt"Forwarded by the destination service into the exchange request body. Without it IAS issues an opaque token that the DAS app router rejects with a generic 400 Bad Request.
tokenService.addClientCredentialsInBody = "true"Sends client_id/client_secret in the form body instead of as Basic Auth. Several IAS app configurations only accept the body variant for the JWT-bearer grant.
x_user_token.jwks_uriThe JWKS endpoint of the IAS tenant that issued the inbound user token. The destination service uses it to validate the X-user-token before exchanging. Missing this is the root cause of the unexpected error — there's no other diagnostic.

Calling the destination

With the destination in place, getting the callback token is two HTTP calls:

# 1. Service token for the Destination Service (XSUAA, client credentials)
DEST_TOKEN=$(curl -s -X POST "$DEST_TOKEN_URL" \
  --user "$DEST_CLIENT_ID:$DEST_CLIENT_SECRET" \
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials" | jq -r .access_token)

# 2. Find the destination, passing the inbound user JWT as X-user-token
curl -s -X GET "$DEST_SERVICE_URL/destination-configuration/v1/destinations/JOULE_ASYNC_API" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $DEST_TOKEN" \
  -H "X-user-token: $INBOUND_USER_JWT"

Here we do a toy example with curl. The response carries the resolved destination plus the exchanged token under authTokens[0]:

{
  "destinationConfiguration": {
    "URL": "https://sapdas.<region>.sapdas.cloud.sap/api/agent/v1/callback/a2a",
    "...": "..."
  },
  "authTokens": [
    {
      "type": "Bearer",
      "value": "eyJqa3UiOi...",
      "http_header": {
        "key": "Authorization",
        "value": "Bearer eyJqa3UiOi..."
      },
      "expires_in": "3600"
    }
  ]
}

Decoding the JWT in authTokens[0].value shows the user identity still in there — sap_id_type: "user", the user's email, ias_apis: ["joule-api"], and the aud set to the Joule app. And this is exactly what we need.

Posting the callback

We can also test the callback directly with curl:

curl -X POST "$(jq -r .destinationConfiguration.URL <<<"$DEST_RESPONSE")" \
  -H "Authorization: $(jq -r '.authTokens[0].http_header.value' <<<"$DEST_RESPONSE")" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "conversationid: <FROM_INBOUND_REQUEST>" \
  -H "x-correlationid: <FROM_INBOUND_REQUEST>" \
  -d '{
    "id": "<x-correlationid value>",
    "jsonrpc": "2.0",
    "result": {
      "id": "<taskId>",
      "status": {
        "state": "completed",
        "message": {
          "messageId": "msg-001",
          "kind": "message",
          "role": "agent",
          "parts": [{"kind": "text", "text": "Status message"}]
        }
      },
      "contextId": "<contextId>",
      "kind": "task",
      "artifacts": [
        {
          "artifactId": "result-001",
          "parts": [{"kind": "text", "text": "Final result for the user"}]
        }
      ]
    }
  }'

The body wraps the A2A Task in a JSON-RPC envelope. The result.status.state can be working (intermediate update), input-required (multi-turn), or completed (done). The result.artifacts carry the final answer that ends up in the Joule UI.

Putting it in Python

Two small things change vs. the multi-turn agent — everything else (agent.py, the agent card, manifest) stays the same.

a2a_multiturn_async/app/
├── agent.py               # unchanged
├── agent_executor.py      # CHANGED — fire-and-forget background task
├── joule_client.py        # NEW    — destination-service exchange + envelope builders
├── app.py                 # unchanged shape (just push_notifications=True on the AgentCard)
└── manifest.yaml

The full code is on GitHub — here I'll just call out the bits that are actually new.

agent_executor.py — return immediately, push later

The synchronous executor would await the agent's stream and enqueue events. The async one schedules the work as a background asyncio task and returns right away, so the SDK can answer Joule's HTTP request with a 200. The user JWT and the two correlation headers come straight off the inbound RequestContext — same trick as in the principal-propagation post:

# agent_executor.py — only the new bits
def _extract_request_state(context: RequestContext) -> tuple[str, str, str]:
    headers = context._call_context.state.get("headers", {}) if context._call_context else {}
    auth = headers.get("authorization", "")
    user_jwt = auth[len("Bearer "):] if auth.lower().startswith("bearer ") else ""
    return user_jwt, headers.get("conversationid", ""), headers.get("x-correlationid", "")


async def execute(self, context, event_queue):
    task = context.current_task or new_task(context.message)
    await event_queue.enqueue_event(task)

    user_jwt, conversation_id, correlation_id = _extract_request_state(context)

    # Decouple the long-running work from the inbound request — SDK returns 200
    # immediately, results come back through Joule's async callback URL.
    bg = asyncio.create_task(
        self._push_results(task, context.get_user_input(),
                           user_jwt, conversation_id, correlation_id),
    )
    self._background_tasks.add(bg)
    bg.add_done_callback(self._background_tasks.discard)

Two things worth flagging:

  • Hold on to the background task. asyncio.create_task returns a weakly-referenced task; if you don't keep a reference, the GC can collect it mid-flight. The _background_tasks set is the standard fix.
  • Why not the SDK's BasePushNotificationSender? The A2A SDK ships one, and it does the right thing for vanilla A2A — DefaultRequestHandler calls it automatically when the executor emits task events, and it POSTs task.model_dump() to the registered pushNotificationConfig.url. Two reasons it doesn't fit Joule's flavour out of the box: (1) Joule expects the task wrapped in a JSON-RPC envelope ({id, jsonrpc, result: <task>}), not the bare task; (2) the callback needs a destination-service-exchanged Bearer plus the original conversationid / x-correlationid headers — none of which are reachable from inside _dispatch_notification, because by then the inbound RequestContext is gone. You can bridge that with a Starlette middleware + ContextVar, but pushing directly from the executor — which still has the full request state — keeps it simpler.

The _push_results body just iterates agent.stream(...) and calls joule_client.post_callback(...) for each step, picking the right envelope (working / input-required / completed) based on the step type.

joule_client.py — destination service + JSON-RPC envelopes

This is the only fully new file. Two responsibilities, kept side by side:

1. Destination-service exchange. Same shape as s4_client.py from the principal propagation post — get a service token, then resolve the destination with X-user-token. The destination service runs the JWT-bearer exchange against IAS for us:

# joule_client.py
async def _resolve_destination(http, user_jwt):
    dest_token = await _get_dest_service_token(http)   # client-credentials
    r = await http.get(
        f"{DEST_SERVICE_URL}/destination-configuration/v1/destinations/{DEST_NAME}",
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {dest_token}", "X-user-token": user_jwt},
    )
    r.raise_for_status()
    return r.json()   # carries authTokens[0].value = exchanged Joule-API JWT

2. JSON-RPC envelope builders. Joule expects the A2A Task wrapped in JSON-RPC, with a state of working, input-required, or completed. Three tiny helpers cover all three:

def completed_envelope(task_id, context_id, correlation_id, message):
    """Final result — `artifacts` is what shows up in Joule as the assistant message."""
    return {
        "id": correlation_id or task_id,
        "jsonrpc": "2.0",
        "result": {
            "id": task_id, "contextId": context_id, "kind": "task",
            "status": {"state": "completed"},
            "artifacts": [{
                "artifactId": f"result-{task_id}",
                "parts": [{"kind": "text", "text": message}],
            }],
        },
    }
# working_envelope / input_required_envelope are the same shape with a different status block

And the actual POST — pull URL + Authorization header out of the destination response, echo conversationid and x-correlationid so Joule routes the result to the right conversation:

async def post_callback(http, user_jwt, conversation_id, correlation_id, body):
    dest = await _resolve_destination(http, user_jwt)
    auth_tokens = dest.get("authTokens") or []
    callback_url = dest["destinationConfiguration"]["URL"]
    headers = {
        auth_tokens[0]["http_header"]["key"]: auth_tokens[0]["http_header"]["value"],
        "Content-Type": "application/json",
        "conversationid": conversation_id,
        "x-correlationid": correlation_id,
    }
    return await http.post(callback_url, json=body, headers=headers)

That's it for the moving parts: a few extra lines in the executor to fork off the work, plus one client module for the destination-service dance and the envelope shape. The agent itself doesn't even know it's running async.

app.py — one flag on the agent card

The only change in app.py is on the AgentCard:

capabilities=AgentCapabilities(streaming=True, push_notifications=True),

No middleware, no custom push sender wired into the request handler — just the standard DefaultRequestHandler with InMemoryTaskStore. The async behaviour is fully encapsulated in the executor.

manifest.yaml — the destination-service binding

The only new env block on top of the multi-turn manifest is the destination-service credentials (so joule_client.py can call it):

env:
  DEST_SERVICE_URL:    "https://destination-configuration.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com"
  DEST_TOKEN_URL:      "https://<subaccount>.authentication.<region>.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token"
  DEST_CLIENT_ID:      "<from dest-service service-key>"
  DEST_CLIENT_SECRET:  "<from dest-service service-key>"
  DEST_NAME:           "JOULE_ASYNC_API"

Status updates and timeout rules

A few rules of thumb:

  • Short tasks: status updates are optional. You can stay quiet and just push the final completed task at the end.
  • Long-running tasks: send an intermediate working status update periodically to keep the conversation alive in Joule's view — something like "Still computing exchange rates…". I tested this with updates every ~30 seconds and ran a single task well past 10 minutes without Joule timing out. The exact thresholds are up to the Joule platform and may change over time, so don't hard-code them — just push heartbeats often enough to be safe.
  • States you'll use: working (still going), input-required (multi-turn — pause and ask the user), completed (done — artifact lands in the UI).

Multi-turn composes cleanly with async: an input-required push notification will show up in Joule UI as a question to the user, and the user's reply comes back as a normal A2A request with the same contextId and taskId.

Testing the async response

Sending convert 5 usd to eur from Joule and watching the CF logs of the agent shows the full async lifecycle in five lines:

agent_executor: configuration=PushNotificationConfig(
   url='https://sapdas.<region>.sapdas.cloud.sap/api/agent/v1/callback/a2a')
httpx: HTTP Request: GET  https://api.frankfurter.dev/v1/latest?from=USD&to=EUR  → 200 OK
httpx: HTTP Request: POST https://<ias-tenant>.accounts.ondemand.com/oauth2/token → 200
token_exchange: Token exchanged for Joule async-api JWT (expires_in=3600s)
httpx: HTTP Request: POST https://sapdas.<region>.sapdas.cloud.sap/api/agent/v1/callback/a2a → 200 OK
push_sender: Push notification sent for task_id=029b5be9-… to https://sapdas.<region>.sapdas.cloud.sap/api/agent/v1/callback/a2a

Inbound request carries the pushNotificationConfig, the agent does its work, the inbound user token is exchanged for a Joule-API JWT, and the callback is posted with 200 OK from the DAS app router.

And on the Joule UI side:

 

The user message goes in, the agent acks immediately (Joule shows a "working" indicator), and the final result lands in the conversation as a regular assistant message — no different from the synchronous case, just decoupled in time.

Conclusion

With async A2A in place, the design space for Joule-integrated agents opens up considerably. You're no longer constrained to "must finish within an HTTP timeout" — your agent can take its time, do real work, hit slow systems, wait for humans, and still deliver the result through the same conversational interface. In general, agents are running longer and longer, and patterns are becoming relevant where execution is rather system-triggered with feedback not in minutes but hours, days or weeks later. For those cases the architecture would shift in general from such a conversational agent to one running in the background.

Find the full code on GitHub under a2a_multiturn_async/.

Hope you enjoyed the post and feel free to leave a comment.

entry.title: Provisioning users for Selective access Joule Pilot on SAP Concur
entry.content:
SAP Concur allows using "Selective Access" for Joule, which enables you to test the SAP's Gen AI capabilities in your production environment using a restricted Joule Pilot User Group before a full, organization-wide rollout.

The “Selective Access” mechanism enables a controlled user experience, which is a key design principle for introducing AI features like Joule in enterprise applications.

Instead of enabling Joule for the entire organization at once, SAP Concur allows administrators to:

  • Restrict access to a predefined subset of users
  • Gradually introduce and validate AI functionality
  • Ensure that the feature behaves as expected under real production conditions

Once the solution has already been tested in production, the organization can proceed with higher confidence, validate business impact, better user acceptance. 

Pre-requisites :

Pilot Mode option in the Joule configuration settings at the company level must be enabled for Concur. Refer Enable Joule Pilot Mode for Your Company | SAP Help Portal

 

 

User Provisioning :

We would be focusing mostly on the provisioning part of the Joule setup which is required for selective access based on a predefined group.

There are 2 approaches - a fully automated end to end approach and a partially automated approach.

Set Up Pilot User Group — Option A vs. Option B

 Option A: Retrieve from ConcurOption B: Manual Creation in CIS
When to useConcur is already your user source and the group exists thereYou want to create and manage the pilot group directly in CIS (IAS)
Group originPulled from Concur → IAS via IPSCreated manually in IAS, then pushed to Concur

Option A — Retrieve group from Concur

  1. IPS Config (Concur → IAS): Set Concur as Source, IAS/Local Directory as Target. Extend transformation to include group replication and set filter: concur.group.filter = displayName eq Joule Pilot User
  2. Add Pilot Users in IAS: Navigate to Users & Authorizations → Groups → Joule Pilot User → add users.
  3. IPS config sync back (IAS → Concur): Set IAS as Source, Concur as Target. Filter with dds.group.filter = displayName eq Joule Pilot User. Run IPS job — group membership now controls Joule access automatically.

 Option B — Manual Creation of group in CIS

  1. Create Group manually in IAS – Joule Pilot User:
  2. Add Users: Assign pilot users to the group.
  3. IPS Config (LID/IAS -> Concur): Set Local Identity Directory as Source with filter on users assigned to user group "Joule Pilot User"
  4. IPS Config (Concur as Target): Set Concur as Target System with the standard user/group transformation → run IPS job to provision pilot user assignments to Concur.

 

We will walkthrough the simpler Option B where we would manually create ‘Joule Pilot User’ in CIS and then sync just the users assignments from IAS to Concur using IPS job. There are some pain points regarding connectivity to Concur which would be covered. 

However, if you want to setup or already have connection from Concur to IAS then you can also go with the option A. For the pilot involving just 1 group, option B is easier and faster.

1. Go to Users & Authorizations → Groups → create group named exactly ‘Joule Pilot User’ in IAS

2. Assign pilot users to the group – Joule Pilot User

Click ‘Add’ button in the group and search and add all the users required for this pilot.

 

3. IPS configuration for source system:

Go to Identity Provisioning -> Source systems

Configure Local Identity Directory as Source System

  1. Save and keep the default transformation
  2. In properties add:
NameValue
idds.group.filterdisplayName eq "Joule Pilot User"

This is important so that users are filtered based on the group – Joule Pilot User and not all the users are provisioned.

 

You can add other optional traces in properties.

ips.trace.created.entitytrue
ips.trace.created.entity.contentfalse
ips.trace.deleted.entitytrue
ips.trace.failed.entity.contentfalse
ips.trace.skipped.entitytrue
ips.trace.skipped.entity.contentfalse
ips.trace.updated.entitytrue
ips.trace.updated.entity.contentfalse

If you wish to replicate only limited number of users to Concur, user also filter idds.user.filter in property.

 

4. IPS configuration for Target System:

Go to Identity Provisioning -> Target systems

Configure Concur as Target System

  1. Source System: Local Identity Directory
  2. Replace transformation with below:
{
  "user": {
    "skipOperations": [
      "create",
      "delete"
    ],
    "mappings": [
      {
        "sourceVariable": "entityIdTargetSystem",
        "targetPath": "$.id"
      },
      {
        "constant": [
          "urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:core:2.0:User",
          "urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:User"
        ],
        "targetPath": "$.schemas"
      },
      {
        "sourcePath": "$.userName",
        "targetPath": "$.userName"
      },
      {
        "sourcePath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:User']['userUuid']",
        "optional": true,
        "targetPath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:User']['userUuid']"
      }
    ]
  },

  "group": {
    "ignore": false,
    "condition": "isAttributeWithOptionalPrefix($.displayName, concur.group.prefix) && isAttributeWithOptionalPrefix($['urn:sap:cloud:scim:schemas:extension:custom:2.0:Group']['name'], concur.group.prefix) && (isRegularGroup() || isApplicationSpecificGroup())",
    "mappings": [
      {
        "sourceVariable": "entityIdTargetSystem",
        "targetPath": "$.id"
      },
      {
        "constant": [
          "urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:core:2.0:Group",
          "urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:Group"
        ],
        "targetPath": "$.schemas"
      },
      {
        "sourcePath": "$.displayName",
        "targetPath": "$.displayName",
        "functions": [
          {
            "function": "replaceFirstString",
            "condition": "isAttributeWithMandatoryPrefix(@, concur.group.prefix)",
            "regex": "%concur.group.prefix%",
            "replacement": ""
          }
        ]
      },
      {
        "sourcePath": "$.members[*].value",
        "preserveArrayWithSingleElement": true,
        "optional": true,
        "targetPath": "$.members[?(@.value)]",
        "functions": [
          {
            "function": "resolveEntityIds"
          }
       ]
      }
    ]
  }
}

3. Properties:

Name

Value

Authentication

BasicAuthentication

concur.api.version

3

concur.company.domain

*************

concur.company.id

(Retreive this value from Concur. Steps mentioned below under 4.1. Generate company request token)

concur.datacenter

(Depends on your Concur tenant)

Concur.authorization.code(Credential type)

(Retreive this value from Concur. Steps mentioned below under 4.1. Generate company request token)

concur.support.patch.operation

true

concur.user.unique.attribute

userName

ips.trace.failed.entity.content

true

ProxyType

Internet

Type

HTTP

 

In case you are having SCIM v4 API supported on Concur, then you can put concur.api.version as 3. This is the connector version value. Please do not put 4 or any other value.

Value = 3 → SCIM v4 API

Value = 2 → Identity v4 API

Value = 1 (or not set) → User v1 API (deprecated)

 

4.1. Generate company request token – This part is additionally required to be done by Concur Administrator

4.1.2. In Concur, go to the Company Request Token Self-Service Tool page.

4.1.3.  Enter the App ID below for SAP IPS.

Identity Provider

App ID

SAP IPS

5bea7d57-6bc5-45ba-b5cf-91f04940fbf2

4.1.4.  Click Generate → a "Company Request Token Successfully Created" dialog appears.

4.1.5. Copy & save both values:

  • Company UUID
  • Company Request Token

4.1.6.  Use these two values in the 3. properties mentioned above and map as following:

concur.company.id - Company UUID

Concur.authorization.code - Company Request Token

NOTE : Token expires in 24 hours — if unused, repeat steps 1–4 to get a new one.

 

5.  After the first run of the job, Identity Provisioning fills in automatically a ‘refresh token’ as the value of the concur.refresh.token property. If a provisioning job has not been run for six months, you’ll again need to generate a new token. Below is shown what happens after first with an error.

 

But after the first run you would see that the property concur.authorization.code got replaced by concur.refresh.token. This means that IPS connectivity is established with Concur.

 

6. Now you can run the job for ‘Pilot User Group’ assignment synchronization to Concur and you should be able to see the updates under group.

 

Once all the steps are finalized, the Joule diamond icon becomes visible to authorized users in Concur, granting them full access to Joule's pilot features.

 

 

Conclusion :

By enabling Joule through SAP Concur’s Selective Access approach, organizations can introduce GenAI leveraging real production conditions. The step-by-step setup covers the provisioning activities required. There are a set of activities to be done additionally for connectivity which requires coordination between Concur admin and security admins and the steps have been detailed out. Once the pilot proves successful, organizations can confidently scale Joule adoption, backed by real validations and strong user acceptance.

 

References :

Joule Selective Access: Pilot Enablement | SAP Help Portal

Option B: Manual Creation of Joule Pilot User Group in CIS Tenant | SAP Help Portal

SAP Concur as target in Identity Provisioning SAP Concur | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: Cloud Identity Services Bug in SAP BTP Trial Environment: Current Status
entry.content:
Dear Community,

We would like to provide an update regarding the current Cloud Identity Services issue in the trial environment.

Some users may encounter a “Forbidden” error after completing the initial setup. This is related to a known bug where the required roles are not correctly assigned during the first-time administrator setup.

During further investigation, our team identified that the issue is more complex than initially expected. As a result, the timeline for the fix needs to be extended. Unfortunately, we cannot provide a confirmed resolution date at this point.

Please be assured that our team is continuing to work on this issue. We will update this community post as soon as there are any relevant changes or once a confirmed timeline becomes available.

We sincerely apologize for the continued inconvenience and thank you for your patience and understanding.

entry.title: Check and then Re-Check Your Cloud Foundry Org and Spaces for Correct User Assignments
entry.content:
Summary: A user can have zero access to SAP BTP Global and SubAccount and still be able to create, read, edit, or delete every service key in BTP! All you need is access at the Cloud Foundry level. Your landscape can be compromised leading to data privacy issues and business continuity can be disrupted significantly. Read more to find out how.

Most of the organizations use SAP BTP Integration Suite to connect systems across their landscape. A standard pattern is exposing HTTP endpoints (or other inbound endpoints) to third party systems so they can trigger integration flows.

To authenticate against those endpoints, you create service keys on the Process Integration Runtime service instance (integration-flow). These are basically API credentials in a way which we call Service keys (the other API credentials are available in the API Management - Developer Hub) and they live at the Cloud Foundry space tied to the service instance. Anyone with those credentials can call your integration endpoints directly and send or read data.

 

BTP admins generally manage user access carefully at SubAccount and Global Account level but generally find it too taxing to manage carefully at Cloud Foundry org and space level. When offboarding someone, most admins remove the user from the SubAccount and Global Account either manually or using IAS and then stop there. If that person still has any kind of CF access, they retain full access to every service key -- and they don't need the BTP cockpit to use it. In fact they won't have access to these using the BTP Cockpit. All they need is CF CLI or CF API to access these. The CF CLI and CF API are available to anyone on the internet for download, and a few commands is all it takes to create, read or delete any service key in the BTP environment.

I have found this security hole at many customers I am working with. The team has removed the user from the SubAccount but the user is still active in Cloud Foundry with no one having bothered to remove them from there thinking that removing them from SubAccount using IAS is enough. It is not! Even if you remove someone through IAS you must separately ensure they are fully removed from CF membership, not just their roles.

Concrete Example: say Target orders flow into S/4HANA via Integration Suite secured by service keys. A disgruntled ex-employee with no BTP cockpit access but still active in CF can delete every one of those keys. Integrations go down, orders stop getting received, and the business stops!

SAP does offer a connector to automate CF user and role management via SAP IPS (Identity Provisioning Service): https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/streamlining-user-management-integrating-sap-integration-suite-with-sap/ba-p/14069705

If you have used, please comment and share you experience especially on how easy it is to use it.

A Big thanks to the SAP BTP xID tool because of which I was able to find this issue.

In the meanwhile I urge all SAP BTP Administrators to check their CF Orgs and Spaces for any assigned users who should not be there.

Update: Possibly, the following security standards get violated when you just rely on controlling S user lifetime to control access to the API and Service Keys. 

1. ISO/IEC 27001:2022 (Information Security Management) - Control A.5.15 (Access Control): Requires access to assets to be restricted based on business and security requirements.

2. NIST SP 800-53 (Security and Privacy Controls) - AC-2 (Account Management)

3.  PCI DSS 4.0 (Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard) - Requirement 8.6: Strictly regulates the use of application accounts

4. SOC 2 Type II (Trust Services Criteria) - Logical Access Controls (CC6.1/CC6.2): Internal controls must prevent unauthorized logical access to data.

5.  Sarbanes-Oxley (SOX) Section 404 (Internal Controls) Compliance - Section 404 mandates that management and auditors establish, maintain, and regularly assess the effectiveness of internal controls over financial reporting to prevent fraud and data tampering. Financial data generally passes through these integrations on a regular basis. If the keys are freely accessible, it is possible, that a rogue internal or external actor can tamper with integrations, run them with incorrect data with no way to find out later who did it.

My recommendation:  I would recommend that apart from one nominated administrator, nobody else should have access to the Production SAP BTP Cloud Foundry Environment in normal course of business. The access should be given to the CF environment only as temporary firefighter access same as is today done in SAP ERP or S/4HANA Production systems. Developers generally do not have unhindered access in these systems, most of the time not even read only access. The same concept needs to be applied to SAP BTP production environments also. This needs to be matured as part of SAP BTP Governance setup.

entry.title: Ask Me Anything with SAP Champions – Monthly Series (Session 5)
entry.content:
Hi folks, Martin here 👋
 
🎬We're back — and this time we're getting our hands dirty with security.
 
The Ask Me Anything with SAP Champions series is rolling into Session 5, and I'm excited to be in the hot seat this time. The concept is dead simple: you ask, I answer. No slides, no scripted demos, no marketing gloss — just honest answers to the questions you actually care about.
 
And the topic? The one that keeps SAP architects, Basis admins, and CISOs up at night: SAP Security 🛡️
 
Securing S/4HANA, RISE, GROW, BTP, threat detection, identity and access, integration security, and the brand-new headache of AI agents poking around in your SAP data — if it's about keeping SAP safe, it's fair game.

🤔So what should you ask me?

Honestly? Whatever's bugging you. But if you want a head start, here's what fits this session perfectly:
 
- 🔐 Securing SAP in the cloud — RISE, S/4HANA, BTP and the works
- 👀 Threat detection & monitoring — actually *seeing* what's happening inside SAP
- 🪪 Identity & access — SSO, principal propagation, federation, least privilege done right
- 🔗 Integration security — APIs, OData, BTP, connecting SAP to the outside world without leaving the door open
- 🤖 AI & automation — keeping copilots and agents that read SAP data on a tight leash
- 🚀 Getting started — practical first steps when you're maturing your SAP security posture

 

Don't see your topic? Ask anyway. The questions I have to *think* about are always the best ones — and far more fun than the easy ones. 😉

📨How to send me your questions (pick whatever's easiest)

Three ways in, all roads lead to the same place:
 
1. Microsoft Forms — name or community handle optional, ask anonymously if you like 👉 link
2. Comment right here — drop your question under this blog post
3. LinkedIn — reply to my announcement post 👉 [LinkedIn post link — placeholder]

 

🗓️ Get them in by: July 6th

🎥What happens next

Here's the neat part — there's nothing to attend, nothing to squeeze into your calendar:

 

1. You send your questions before the deadline.
2. I pick the most interesting and widely useful ones.
3. I answer them on camera in a recorded video on the SAP Community YouTube channel 📺
4. And for the readers among you (I see you 👀), a written recap blog with all the questions and answers lands right here afterwards.
 
So whether you prefer to watch or read, you get the full thing on your own schedule. 🙌

💡Why bother joining in?

- Get a real answer to a real problem — bring me the thing you're genuinely stuck on.
- Learn from questions you'd never have thought to ask — this community always surfaces angles I love.
- Steer the series — your questions shape what we tackle next.
 

👉Over to you

SAP security isn't a one-and-done checkbox — it's an ongoing conversation. So let's have it.
 
Fire your question over via anonymous Forms, drop it in the comments, or hit me up on [LinkedIn — placeholder] before July 6th.
 
I'll see you in the recording — and in the recap blog right here on the community. Let's go! 🚀
 
See the other series editions in the community corner and where it all began here.
 
#SAPChampions #AMA #SAPCommunity #SAPSecurity #SAPCyberDefense

entry.title: Application Deletion in SAP Identity Access Governance (IAG) – Practical Approach
entry.content:
Introduction

During one of the SAP Identity Access Governance (IAG) implementation projects, I encountered a common challenge related to application cleanup in test systems before UAT.

Typically, we integrate multiple SAP cloud and on-premises applications into the IAG landscape during the initial phases in the test environment, followed by reconfiguration in production after successful completion of UT, SIT, and UAT cycles.

In real project scenarios, however, the test system often ends up with additional applications created for temporary purposes such as proof of concept (POC), testing, or initial integration validation.

 

Business Challenge

Before starting UAT, clients expect a clean IAG environment without unnecessary:

  • Applications
  • Access/request data
  • Business roles
  • Users and role assignments

However, earlier SAP guidance (for example, SAP Note 2817560) indicated that applications could not be deleted once used, primarily due to audit considerations.

This created a challenge in maintaining a clean and controlled UAT environment.

 

Solution Overview

SAP has now addressed this limitation by introducing a role collection:

CIAG_Connector_Deletion_Administration

This role collection enables administrators to delete applications along with all associated data in SAP IAG.

 

Step-by-Step Approach

Step 1: Clean Up Risk Analysis Data

Before deleting an application, it is important to remove risk analysis dependencies.

  • Navigate to Administration → Applications tile
  • Select the relevant application
  • Remove the Business Function Group association

  • Run the "Access Analysis" job via Job Scheduler

This ensures removal of

  • Role-level risk analysis results
  • User-level risk analysis results

Note: This step does not delete the ruleset or business function group itself. If required, those can be deleted separately from the Business Function Groups tile.

 

Step 2: Delete the Application and Associated Data

  • Navigate to Administration → Applications tile
  • Select the application to be deleted
  • Click on Delete button at bottom right corner

  • Enter the application name for confirmation
  • Click Delete button

System Behavior

  • A message appears indicating deletion is in progress
  • After completion, the application is removed from the list
  • On refresh, the application is no longer visible

You can validate the cleanup through

  • Maintain User Data → to check user-related data
  • Access Maintenance → to verify role/access cleanup

 

Important Considerations

  • Avoid mapping this role collection to SAP IAS groups that are requestable via IAG, in case SAP IAS is also one of the target applications
  • Do not assign this role in production environments, as production data should not be deleted to comply with audit requirements.
  • In test systems:
    • Assign only temporarily
    • Remove immediately after use to prevent accidental deletion

 

Key learnings

  • Application deletion in SAP IAG is now possible with controlled authorization
  • Proper cleanup (risk data, associations) is critical before deletion
  • This feature is especially useful for:
    • UAT preparation
    • Cleaning up POC integrations
    • Maintaining governance in test systems

entry.title: Restricting User Access in SAP SuccessFactors via IAS & IPS Without Making the User Inactive
entry.content:
What This Blog Covers: A real-world implementation where a global organisation needed to restrict specific country-based users from accessing SAP SuccessFactors during a phased go-live rollout. We cover the complete journey — architecture, IPS transformation rules, IAS group management, conditional authentication, troubleshooting 10 real issues encountered, and how to cleanly remove restrictions post go-live. All sensitive information has been masked for publication.

1. Background and Business Requirement

During a phased go-live of SAP SuccessFactors (Recruiting, Onboarding, and Benefits modules), the requirement was to temporarily restrict users from a specific country entity from accessing the instance — while allowing all other global users to continue their normal SSO login via Microsoft Azure AD.

Key requirements:

#

Requirement

1

Restrict specific users based on their company/entity assignment in SuccessFactors

2

Keep restricted users active in IAS — no account deactivation

3

Non-restricted users must continue SSO via Azure AD without any disruption

4

Restriction must be temporary and easily reversible post Go-Live

5

Support both restricting all users in an entity AND specific named users

6

Work within the existing IAS + IPS + Azure AD SSO architecture

2. Solution Architecture

The solution connects SAP SuccessFactors, IPS, IAS, and Microsoft Azure AD. SAP SuccessFactors is the master source of users. IPS provisions users and group membership into IAS. IAS controls authentication routing and access decisions.

Component

Role in Solution

SAP SuccessFactors

Master source of users. Company/entity field (custom02) identifies restricted users.

IPS Source System

Reads users from SF, applies transformation rules, tags restricted users with IAS group

IPS Target System

Writes user attributes and group membership to IAS

IAS

Identity Provider for SP-initiated SSO. Applies access restriction rules.

Microsoft Azure AD

Corporate IdP used for Employee, Partner and Contractor SSO

IAS Group

Tags restricted users — referenced by Conditional Auth and Risk Based Auth rules

3. Solution Design — Three Layer Approach

The restriction requires all three layers working together. Removing any single layer causes the restriction to fail silently.

Critical: Without the Conditional Authentication Priority 1 rule, restricted users match the existing Employee/Partner rules and are routed directly to Azure AD — completely bypassing the Risk Based Auth DENY rule. All three layers are mandatory.

4. IPS Configuration

4.1 Identify the Company Field

The company/entity field in SF is mapped as custom02 in the IPS source transformation. The exact raw value must be verified from IPS trace logs — not the display label shown in the SF UI. Enable ips.trace.skipped.entity.content = true in IPS Source System Properties to see exact raw field values in provisioning logs.

Important: Always verify that custom02 is listed in the sf.user.attributes property of the IPS Source System. If missing, the SF OData API will never return this field and all custom02 conditions will silently fail with no error message shown.

4.2 Source Transformation — Three Approaches

Option A — Restrict all users in an entity (company field):

{    "condition": "$.custom02 == 'YOUR_COMPANY_CODE_VALUE'",    "constant": "Restricted_Users_Group",    "optional": true,    "targetPath": "$.groups[0].value"}

Option B — Restrict specific User IDs only:

{    "condition": "$.userId == 'USER_ID_1' || $.userId == 'USER_ID_2' || $.userId == 'USER_ID_3'",    "constant": "Restricted_Users_Group",    "optional": true,    "targetPath": "$.groups[0].value"}

Option C — Company entity with named exclusions (Recommended for large user sets):

{    "condition": "$.custom02 == 'YOUR_COMPANY_CODE_VALUE' && $.userId != 'EXCLUDE_USER_1' && $.userId != 'EXCLUDE_USER_2'",    "constant": "Restricted_Users_Group",    "optional": true,    "targetPath": "$.groups[0].value"}

Recommendation: Option C is best when you have a large number of users to restrict but a smaller exclusion list. It automatically applies to new hires in the entity and only requires maintaining the exclusion list — much shorter and more maintainable than listing hundreds of individual user IDs.

Note on userId: The userId field in IPS maps to the SF User ID field — NOT the Username or Login Name. These are completely different values. The userId is often numeric (e.g. 1103908). Always verify in SF Admin Center → Manage Users before using in conditions.

4.3 Target Transformation — Groups Passthrough Mapping

Add the following as the last mapping in the user section of the IAS target transformation (after the passwordStatus or sendMail mappings):

{    "sourcePath": "$.groups[*].value",    "preserveArrayWithSingleElement": true,    "optional": true,    "targetPath": "$.groups[?(@.value)]"} // Note: resolveEntityIds intentionally NOT used here.// Manually created IAS groups require direct passthrough.// resolveEntityIds silently drops group assignment when UUID not in IPS cache.

Do NOT use resolveEntityIds for manually created IAS groups. It silently drops the group assignment when the UUID is not in the IPS cache — no error is shown in provisioning logs.

4.4 Critical IPS Properties to Verify

Property

Required Value

Why It Matters

sf.user.attributes

Must include custom02

Without this, SF OData API never returns the field to IPS

sf.user.filter

status eq 'active'

Never filter to single user — causes mass deletion of all other IAS users

ips.trace.skipped.entity.content

true (for debugging)

Shows exact raw field values in provisioning logs

ips.trace.failed.entity.content

true (for debugging)

Shows full error details for failed entity provisioning

4.5 IPS Job Execution Sequence

Always follow this exact sequence. Skipping or reversing the order causes group assignment to fail silently with no error message.

Step

Action

Navigation

Purpose

1

Run READ job on IAS Source System

IPS → Source Systems → [IAS source system] → Jobs → Run Now → READ

Populates IPS internal UUID cache for IAS groups including the restriction group

2

Run RESYNC job on SF Source System

IPS → Source Systems → [SF source system] → Jobs → Run Now → RESYNC

Reads from SF AND writes to IAS. A READ-only job never writes to the IAS target

3

Verify Provisioning Logs

IPS → Provisioning Logs

Confirm restricted users show SUCCESS status

6. End-to-End Flow

Restricted Country User Login Attempt

Step

Event

1

User hits SF URL (SP-initiated SSO)

2

SF redirects to IAS

3

Conditional Auth Priority 1: User Group = Restricted_Users_Group → MATCH

4

Routed to: Identity Authentication (IAS native login page)

5

Risk Based Auth Priority 1: Group = Restricted_Users_Group → DENY

6

Result: "Sorry, we could not authenticate you." — user is BLOCKED from SF, user remains ACTIVE in IAS

Non-Restricted User Login

Step

Event

1

User hits SF URL (SP-initiated SSO)

2

SF redirects to IAS

3

Conditional Auth Priority 1: Restricted_Users_Group → NO MATCH

4

Priority 2/3/4: Employee / Partner / External → Corporate IdP (Azure AD)

5

Result: Normal SSO login via Azure AD — zero impact on non-restricted users

 

User Type

Conditional Auth

Routed To

Risk Based Auth

Final Result

Restricted country user

Blocked — cannot authenticate

Non-restricted user

Azure AD (Corporate IdP)

Not evaluated

Normal SSO login success

7. Troubleshooting — Real Issues Encountered

Every issue below was encountered and resolved during the actual implementation. These are documented to help others avoid the same pitfalls.

#

Issue

Root Cause

Resolution

1

Group not populated in IAS after Resync

Groups passthrough mapping missing from target transformation user section

Add groups passthrough mapping in USER section of IAS target transformation

2

Groups mapping silently failing

resolveEntityIds cannot find UUID for manually created group

Remove resolveEntityIds from the groups mapping entirely

3

DENY rule not triggering

Conditional Auth routing user to Azure AD — DENY rule bypassed entirely

Add Priority 1 Conditional Auth rule routing restricted group to IAS native login first

4

User filter causing mass deletion in IAS

sf.user.filter set to single user — IPS flagged all other users for deletion

Always keep sf.user.filter = status eq 'active' — never filter to single user

5

READ job not updating IAS group

READ job only caches data internally — never writes to IAS target

Use RESYNC job which reads from SF AND writes to IAS target

6

userId condition not matching users

SF userId is often numeric — not the username or login name field

Verify exact userId in SF Admin Center → Manage Users before using in conditions

7

custom02 condition always fails

custom02 not listed in sf.user.attributes property — SF never returns the field

Add custom02 to sf.user.attributes in IPS Source System Properties

8

custom02 value mismatch

SF UI display name differs from the actual OData API raw value returned

Enable trace logging and check exact raw value in IPS provisioning logs

9

Password prompt instead of Access Denied

Risk Based Auth DENY evaluates only after the login attempt is submitted

Expected behaviour — users cannot authenticate regardless of password entered

10

IAS group still populated after removing IPS condition

IPS does not remove group membership when condition is removed — only adds or updates

Manually remove all members from IAS group OR delete the group entirely

8. Removing the Restriction (Post Go-Live)

Important: Removing the IPS condition does NOT automatically remove users from the IAS group. IPS only adds or updates group membership — it never deletes it. Manual removal of IAS group members is always required when lifting a restriction.

Step

Action

Details

1

Remove Group Rule from IPS Source Transformation

Delete the entire condition mapping block from the source transformation. Save the transformation.

2

Run IPS Jobs in Sequence

READ on IAS source → RESYNC on SF source. IPS will no longer re-add users to the group since the condition has been removed.

3

Manually Remove Group Members in IAS

IAS → Users & Authorizations → User Groups → Restricted_Users_Group → select all members → Remove. Alternatively, delete the group entirely if no longer needed.

4

Delete IAS Rules from All Applications

For every SF application in IAS, remove the Conditional Authentication Priority 1 rule AND the Risk Based Authentication DENY rule.

5

Verify SSO Works for Previously Restricted Users

Test 3–5 previously restricted users — they should now be redirected to Azure AD and login successfully via normal SSO.

9. Key Lessons Learned

#

Lesson

Why It Matters

1

Always run READ on IAS source BEFORE Resync

Populates IPS UUID cache needed for group resolution

2

Always use RESYNC job — not READ job

READ job only caches data internally and never writes to IAS target

3

Never filter sf.user.filter to a single user

Causes mass deletion of all other users in IAS on next Resync

4

No resolveEntityIds for manually created IAS groups

Silently drops group assignment with no error shown in logs

5

Conditional Auth Priority 1 rule is mandatory

Without it Azure AD authenticates users before DENY rule can fire

6

DENY rule belongs in Risk Based Auth — not Conditional Auth

Conditional Auth only routes users to IdPs — it cannot deny access

7

SF userId can be numeric — always verify exact value

The userId and Username fields are completely different in SF

8

custom02 must be listed in sf.user.attributes

Field never returned by SF OData API if not explicitly listed

9

IAS group name is case sensitive

Must match the IPS transformation constant value exactly

10

Removing IPS condition does not remove IAS group membership

Manual removal from IAS group is always required after lifting restriction

11

Groups mapping must be in USER section of target transformation

Group section may be set to ignore:true — placing mapping there has no effect

12

Use custom02 + exclusion list for large user sets

Shorter condition, auto-applies to new hires, much easier long-term maintenance

Summary: Restricting access in SAP SuccessFactors via IAS and IPS requires a three-layer approach — IPS transformation tags users with a restriction group, IAS Conditional Authentication routes them to IAS native login at Priority 1, and IAS Risk Based Authentication DENY rule blocks access. Users remain active in IAS throughout. The most critical insight: Conditional Authentication and Risk Based Authentication must work together — one routes the user, the other denies them. Neither works alone for this use case. When lifting the restriction, always manually remove members from the IAS group as removing the IPS condition alone does not trigger group membership deletion.

This blog documents a real implementation with real challenges encountered and resolved. Every troubleshooting point above was an actual issue from the project. I hope this helps others working on similar access restriction requirements with SAP SuccessFactors, IAS, and IPS. Feel free to leave questions in the comments!
